symptoms.symptom_name: Exertional presyncope with near-loss of consciousness
symptoms.character: sudden dizziness and near-blackout while walking
symptoms.onset: acute onset during exertion today, against a background of escalating exertional light-headedness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exertional syncope/presyncope is one limb of the classic SAD triad and is a marker of a major prognostic inflection point in aortic stenosis, mandating urgent evaluation.
Presyncope alone is non-specific and commonly benign, but the exertional (not positional) trigger argues against a simple vasovagal mechanism.
Exertional arrhythmia can cause presyncope, though absence of preceding palpitations reduces but does not exclude this.

symptoms.symptom_name: Exertional angina, triggered by walking and relieved by rest within 10 minutes

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exertional angina is a limb of the SAD triad, reflecting demand ischaemia against a hypertrophied ventricle with fixed outflow obstruction — average survival without intervention falls to roughly 3 years once angina develops.
Exertional chest tightness in an older patient must independently trigger active ACS exclusion regardless of the eventual structural diagnosis.
Concurrent exertional angina is not a feature of simple vasovagal or orthostatic syncope.

symptoms.symptom_name: Progressive exertional dyspnoea over three months
symptoms.onset: gradual onset, progressive over approximately three months

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Exertional dyspnoea is the third limb of the SAD triad, reflecting rising left ventricular filling pressures and evolving decompensation — average survival without intervention falls to roughly 2 years once heart failure symptoms develop.
A three-month progressive exertional breathlessness trajectory is not explained by an isolated vasovagal event.

symptoms.symptom_name: Recurrent exertional light-headedness in the weeks preceding today's episode

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An escalating pattern of exertional presyncope rather than a single isolated event indicates worsening fixed-output physiology and heightens near-term risk.

symptoms.symptom_name: No palpitations preceding the presyncopal episode

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of preceding palpitations makes a primary exertional arrhythmia somewhat less likely as the dominant cause, though it does not exclude it without an ECG.

symptoms.symptom_name: Older adult age — risk modifier for degenerative calcific aortic stenosis

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Degenerative calcific aortic stenosis is strongly age-related, with prevalence rising sharply in older adults; described in the source as frail/elderly.

symptoms.symptom_name: Harsh ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotids (clinician examination finding)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A harsh ejection systolic murmur loudest at the aortic area radiating to the carotids is the classic auscultatory finding of aortic stenosis; cannot be obtained via telehealth chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Slow-rising, small-volume carotid pulse with narrowed pulse pressure (clinician examination finding)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pulsus parvus et tardus with narrowed pulse pressure reflects the fixed, obstructed outflow tract; a classic confirmatory sign requiring physical palpation, unobtainable via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Transthoracic echocardiogram — valve leaflet excursion and transvalvular gradient (investigation result)

investigation_details.expected_result: Reduced aortic valve leaflet excursion with elevated transvalvular peak velocity/gradient, consistent with severe aortic stenosis — exact severity grading requires the formal report.
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Severity of aortic stenosis cannot be quantified and valve replacement candidacy cannot be assessed; the telehealth AI Doctor cannot order this directly but should recommend it as part of urgent in-person workup.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Echocardiography is the definitive investigation for diagnosing and grading aortic stenosis severity.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Syncope, angina, and dyspnoea together form the classic symptomatic triad of severe aortic stenosis. Onset of any one symptom marks a major prognostic turning point; the presence of all three together in this case indicates advanced, high-risk disease with substantial short-term risk of sudden cardiac death.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Mandatory first-line investigation in any presentation combining exertional chest tightness and presyncope, to help exclude concurrent acute coronary syndrome or arrhythmia.

Definitive investigation to confirm and grade aortic valve stenosis severity and guide urgency of cardiology referral and potential valve replacement; cannot be performed via telehealth but should be recommended urgently.
Reasonable baseline bloods in an unwell older adult with presyncope, but not the priority investigation for this presentation.
Not first-line when a structural valvular cause is already clinically suspected from the symptom triad; echocardiography and in-person cardiovascular examination take priority.
